Swiss CitiesZermatt

Zermatt

VSValais

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The race to the top

Two rival mountain goats — one from the Swiss side, one from the Italian side — race up the Matterhorn, mirroring the famous Whymper-Carrel rivalry of 1865. Through tumbles and teamwork, they learn that the mountain is big enough for both and that reaching the top means nothing without friends.

Create This Story

The Matterhorn (4,478 m) was the last great Alpine peak to be climbed. For years, English engraver Edward Whymper and Italian mountaineer Jean-Antoine Carrel competed to be first. On 14 July 1865, Whymper’s team of seven reached the summit via the Hörnli Ridge at 1:40 pm. From the top they could see Carrel’s Italian party still 200 metres below. On the descent, four climbers fell to their deaths when a rope snapped. Carrel’s team reached the summit three days later.

The rope that remembers

A child visits the Matterhorn Museum and touches the broken rope from 1865. It whispers the story of the seven climbers — the joy at the summit, the terrible fall, and the question of blame. Through the rope’s memory, the child relives the most dramatic moment in Alpine history.

Create This Story

The Matterhorn Museum Zermatlantis, opened in 2006 on Kirchplatz, is built almost entirely underground. Its most powerful artefact is the original broken rope from the first ascent of 14 July 1865, along with personal effects of the climbers who perished. The subterranean space recreates original Walser houses, tracing Zermatt’s transformation from a mountain farming village to a world-famous resort. Approximately 40,000 visitors per year explore the museum.

The silent village

A child arrives in Zermatt and notices something strange: no car noise. The village’s tiny electric taxis tell the story of how they replaced horses in the 1980s, why clean air matters when you live at the foot of the Matterhorn, and what a community can achieve when it decides to change.

Create This Story

Zermatt is one of Switzerland’s few completely car-free resort villages — no combustion-engine vehicles are permitted. Citizens voted to ban most cars in 1966. In the early 1980s, the village converted fully to electric vehicles, limited to 4 metres long, 1.4 metres wide, 2 metres high, and 20 km/h. The Imboden family founded STIMBO to manufacture custom electric vehicles. Today approximately 500 electric vehicles serve the entire village.

The little cog train that climbed

The Gornergrat Railway engine is nervous about its very first trip to the top in 1898. A child conductor encourages it past the gorge, through the snowfields, to the 3,089-metre summit — where the breathtaking panorama of 29 peaks makes every turn of the cogwheel worthwhile.

Create This Story

The Gornergrat Railway, opened 20 August 1898, was the first electric cogwheel railway in Switzerland. It climbs from Zermatt (1,620 m) to 3,089 metres in 33 minutes. The 9,339-metre line was built in just two years, employing over 1,000 workers. From the summit, 29 four-thousanders are visible, including the Matterhorn, Monte Rosa, and the Gorner Glacier. It was the highest railway in Europe until the Jungfrau Railway opened in 1912.

The glacier's memory

The Gorner Glacier tells a child about the centuries it has seen — growing during the Little Ice Age, retreating now at 30 metres per year. It asks the child to remember what it once looked like, because glaciers cannot carry their own memories when they melt away.

Create This Story

The Gorner Glacier is the second-largest glacial system in the Alps after the Aletsch Glacier, covering approximately 53 km². It is approximately 12.4 km long and is the source of the Gornera river, which flows through Zermatt. Like almost all Alpine glaciers, it is retreating rapidly — losing approximately 30 metres per year, with a record loss of 290 metres in 2008. The Gornersee, an ice-marginal lake, fills each year and drains dramatically in summer.

The peak that came from Africa

A child learns that the Matterhorn’s summit rock travelled from Africa on a tectonic plate over 100 million years. The mountain tells the story of its extraordinary journey in a single magical night — from the ancient ocean floor to the roof of Europe.

Create This Story

The Matterhorn’s summit is composed of gneiss, a metamorphic rock originating from the African tectonic plate, resting atop younger European rock. The mountain is literally a piece of Africa on top of Europe. Its distinctive pyramid was carved by glacial erosion over millions of years: four cirque glaciers sculpted the four faces. The four ridges — Hörnli, Furggen, Lion, and Zmutt — separate the four faces.

Alexander Seiler and the mountain guests

A boy from the Goms valley sees strangers arriving in Zermatt with ropes and ice axes. Opening a small inn, he discovers that sharing his mountains with the world changes everything — the story of Alexander Seiler and the birth of Alpine tourism.

Create This Story

Alexander Seiler (born 1819), a trained soap and candle maker from the Goms valley, recognised the Alps’ tourism potential early. In 1855, he purchased a wooden chalet and named it Hotel Monte Rosa, converting it into a 35-bed hotel. The Hotel Monte Rosa became the headquarters of pioneering mountaineers including Edward Whymper. By 1890, Seiler controlled nearly every hotel in Zermatt — a total of 1,000 beds and 600 jobs. In 1891, over 30,000 passengers reached Zermatt by the new railway.

The Horu speaks

A child who speaks only standard German arrives in Zermatt and cannot understand anyone. The Matterhorn itself — ds Horu — teaches the child the old Walser words one peak at a time, revealing that language is a living thing shaped by mountains, snow, and centuries of isolation.

Create This Story

Zermatt’s primary language is Walliserdeutsch (Walser German), one of the oldest Alemannic dialects, preserved by centuries of geographic isolation. The dialect is so distinctive that Swiss television sometimes needs subtitles. In local Zermatt dialect, the Matterhorn is called ds Hore or ds Horu, simply meaning the peak. Approximately 10,000 people across the Alps still speak Walser dialect variants today.
